title: Group Invincible: F1TENTH Time Trial
text:
ECE 484 Final Project · F1TENTH Time Trial




Designing a fast, reliable, and competition-ready autonomous race car on a stock F1TENTH platform using onboard sensing, perception, planning, and control.


Zhenyu Zhang, Yuanzhe Wang, Murphy Huang


1University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign · ECE 484 · Spring 2026


 Demo Video  GitHub  System  Results




Goal


Finish the track quickly and cleanly


Optimize for speed, stability, and lap consistency in a one-car time trial setting.




Sensor Stack


LiDAR · Camera · IMU


Use any combination of stock onboard sensors without changing hardware.




Core Challenge


Perception to control under tight latency


Every module must work robustly enough for real-time autonomous driving on a race track.

title: Group Invincible: F1TENTH Time Trial
text:
Overview

We approach the problem as a full autonomy stack. The vehicle uses onboard sensing to understand the nearby environment, converts that information into a track-relative driving objective, and then executes that objective through steering and speed control. The final system design will implement a hybrid strategy with LiDAR and IMU feedback. The final website is structured to show not only the racing result, but also the engineering decisions behind the result: sensing, planning, control logic, evaluation strategy, and lessons learned from real-car testing.

title: Group Invincible: F1TENTH Time Trial
text:
Designed algorism plan


obstacle situation (together as additional interesting point)
Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). We use the 1080-array of LiDAR beams and current velocity to see steering angle and speed. Reward Function for +1 for maintaining high speed, -500 for triggering the TTC collision threshold. Artificial Potential Fields (APF) treats the car like a magnet. Your target destination pulls the car forward as attractive force, but any obstacles detected by the LiDAR push the car away as repulsive force.


Controller (Zhenyu)
For lateral control, we implement the Pure Pursuit algorithm. Simultaneously, longitudinal control is handled by a PID controller, which regulates the car’s throttle. Or Stanley Controller calculates both the cross-track error (how far off the line you are) and the heading error (the angle difference between the car and the track). This prevents the “snaking” or oscillating behavior that Pure Pursuit sometimes suffers from on straightaways.


Real-Time Localization (Yuanzhe)
Our system utilizes Google Cartographer to solve the Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) problem. By performing scan-matching between real-time 2D LiDAR data and IMU inputs, it constructs a high-resolution occupancy grid map of the track. Simultaneously, it provides the vehicle’s precise \((x, y, \theta)\) pose estimate by comparing current sensor inputs against the established global map.


optimal path (Murphy)
For navigation, we implement the A* Algorithm to compute the optimal path through the environment. Operating on the occupancy grid generated by the perception layer, A* employs a heuristic-based search to find the shortest collision-free route from the car’s current position to the target waypoint. This ensures a globally consistent path that accounts for track boundaries and static obstacles.

title: Group Invincible: F1TENTH Time Trial
text:
Sensors and Inputs









Part
Uses What Data
Sends What Next




LiDAR
Distance and obstacle measurements
Range data to perception


Camera
Track images and visual information
Image data to perception


IMU
Motion and orientation data
State data to estimation / control


Perception
LiDAR + camera data
Free-space and track understanding to planning


State Estimation
IMU data
Vehicle pose / heading to planning and control


Planning
Perception + state information
Target path or waypoint to control


Control
Planned target + current vehicle state
Steering and speed commands to the car


Vehicle
Drive commands
New movement and new sensor data

title: Group Invincible: F1TENTH Time Trial
text:
Plan









Step
Main Task
Goal / Output




Step 1
Set up the F1TENTH platform and software environment
Make sure the car, ROS, and sensor drivers run correctly


Step 2
Collect and verify sensor data
Confirm LiDAR, camera, and IMU data can be read reliably


Step 3
Build the perception module
Detect walls, free space, and useful track features


Step 4
Add state estimation
Estimate vehicle heading, motion, and current state


Step 5
Develop the planning module
Choose a safe and efficient path or target waypoint


Step 6
Design the control module
Convert the planned path into steering and speed commands


Step 7
Integrate all modules together
Make sensing, planning, and control run as one full pipeline


Step 8
Test in simulation
Debug the system and tune parameters safely


Step 9
Test on the real car
Verify stable driving on the actual F1TENTH vehicle


Step 10
Improve performance
Reduce lap time, improve smoothness, and increase stability


Step 11
Final evaluation and demo
Record results, compare runs, and prepare the final presentation
